lecture_context.lecture_title: Python Review: Python Basics and NumPy

unit.name: Python motivation and core language basics
unit.description: Introduces the tutorial goal, why Python is used in CS224N-style NLP/deep learning work, and essential Python syntax basics.
unit.summary: The session is framed as a Python and NumPy review for CS224N, especially for homework 2 and later assignments, while still being accessible to students with little programming background [ts=10s]. Python is motivated as a high-level, English-like language with scientific-computing support, efficient math/matrix frameworks through NumPy, and direct interfaces to common deep learning frameworks such as PyTorch and TensorFlow [ts=95s]. Setup material such as package installation and Conda environments is noted but skipped so the lecture can focus on language basics first [ts=132s]. The segment covers dynamic variable assignment, where a variable can be assigned an integer and later reassigned to a string without declaring its type in advance [ts=166s]. It also reviews arithmetic operators, exponentiation with double asterisks, type casting to float/int/string, capitalized Boolean values, None as Python's null-like value distinct from 0, English-like logical operators and/or/not, equality testing with == versus assignment with =, and indentation by consistent spaces or tabs instead of braces [ts=205s] [ts=269s] [ts=361s] [ts=370s].

key_points.text: The tutorial goal is to review Python and NumPy basics needed for homework 2 and later CS224N work.
Python is preferred in ML/NLP because it is high-level, readable, scientifically useful, and integrates with PyTorch and TensorFlow.
Python variables do not require fixed type declarations; the same variable can later hold a value of a different type.
Python supports exponentiation with double asterisks and explicit casting with float(), int(), or str().
Boolean values are capitalized True/False, and None is the null-like value but is not the same as 0.
Use == for equality checking and = for assignment.
Python code blocks are determined by consistent indentation rather than braces; inconsistent indentation throws an error.
